chunk_summary: Guidelines recommend early introduction of allergenic foods like peanut and egg starting at 4–6 months for high-risk infants after clinical evaluation, and around 6 months for others, with safety precautions to prevent choking and manage allergic reactions.
learning_objectives: Understand the risk stratification process for introducing peanut and egg in infants.
Learn the recommended timing and methods for introducing allergenic foods to infants.
Recognize safety precautions and emergency response steps for allergic reactions.
Identify the role of clinical evaluation and testing in high-risk infants.
key_facts: High-risk infants (severe eczema and/or egg allergy) should introduce peanut at 4–6 months after clinical evaluation.
Moderate-risk infants (mild-to-moderate eczema) can introduce peanut around 6 months at home with clinician guidance.
Low-risk infants (no eczema or food allergy) can introduce peanut and egg after starting solids, as per family preference.
Use age-safe forms like thinned peanut butter or peanut powder to avoid choking hazards.
Introduce one allergen at a time during daytime when medical care is accessible.
Emergency symptoms include breathing difficulty, repetitive vomiting with lethargy, facial swelling, or multisystem symptoms.
reference_quote: High-risk infants are those with severe eczema and/or an existing egg allergy. For these infants, introduce age-appropriate peanut-containing foods as early as 4–6 months only after clinical evaluation; testing (peanut-specific IgE or skin prick) may be used to guide whether introduction should be supervised.
document_key_topics: Risk stratification for allergenic food introduction
Timing and methods for introducing peanut and egg
Safety precautions for allergenic food introduction
Management of allergic reactions in infants
